content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 14, 2020

Zachary Wasserman
Wendel Rosen LLP
1111 Broadway, 24th Floor
Oakland, CA 94607

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-20-029

Dear Mr. Wasserman:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Alameda County
Transportation Commission ("ACTC") regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions change, then you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the fact that ACTC's Director of Projects is the beneficiary of a promissory note from
Kimley Horn disqualify him from participation in the process of reviewing submissions if Kimley
Horn submits a bid, or supervising Kimley Horn's work if it is selected by ACTC?

CONCLUSION

Because the Director of Projects has a financial interest in Kimley Horn, he may not
participate in any decisions involving either APHC Inc., the parent company of Kimley Horn and
maker of the promissory note, and Kimley Horn, a wholly owned subsidiary of APCH, Inc.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-20-029
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

ACTC is a joint powers authority which administers the transportation sales tax in Alameda
County and the County Management Agency. County. It is a 22-member
commission with representatives from the Alameda County Board of Supervisors, the
14 cities in Alameda, AC Transit and BART.

As part of its operations, ACTC solicits submissions for engineering services on a variety of
transportation projects and supervises the carrying out of those services. As part of his normal
duties, the Director of Projects ("Director") helps to prepare solicitations and may participate in
reviewing submissions, interviewing firms, and supervising the work of firms engaged by ACTC.
The Director may participate in formulating recommendations to ACTC which are reviewed by the
Deputy Executive Director for Projects and the Executive Director before being submitted to the
Commission for action.

Kimley Horn is one of the firms that frequently submits proposals and is sometimes engaged
to work for ACTC. Prior to working for ACTC approximately one year ago, the Director was
employed by Kimley Horn for over three years and acquired stock in the company. When the
Director left Kimley Horn, he exchanged his stock for a promissory note from APHC Inc., the
parent company of Kimley Horn, in the amount of $152,360 with an interest rate of 9%, to be paid
in five equal installments with the first payment to be made on April 1, 2020. APHC is an
employee-owned company with over 200 employee shareholders and reported revenue in 2018 of
$811 million.

In a subsequent email, you confirmed that Kimley Horn is wholly owned by APHC, Inc.,
and that APHC Inc. is entirely owned by employees. The buyout that the Director received is in
accordance with APHC's governing policies.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of the public official's
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a)).

Section 87103 defines financial interests to include:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

Under Section 82034, an "investment" includes any financial interest in a debt instrument.
In the Holzhaus Advice Letter, No. I-14-218, we said that an official had an investment
interest in his former employer where the employer purchased the official's stock with a

File No. A-20-029
Page No. 3

five-year note because a note is a debt instrument. (See also, the Gallogly Advice Letter, No.
A-17-073, applying the rule to a promissory note.)

•

An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, except gifts or loans
by a commercial lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating $500 or more in value
provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

"For purposes of the Act, a public official's income includes income which as been
promised to the public official at the time he or she has a legally enforceable right to the
promised income." (Regulation 18703.3(a).) Thus, receipt of loan repayments and interest
are included in the definition of "income" for purposes of the Act's disclosure requirements.
(Section 82030; Cahill Advice Letter, No. 76-122; Deddeh Advice Letter, No. 88-180.) The
debtor on the loan would normally be considered a source of income to the official for the
full amount of the loan until the loan is repaid and for twelve months thereafter. (Anderson
Advice Letter, No. A-96-241.)

According to the facts you have provided, the Director received income for the sale of his
interest in Kimley Horn approximately one year ago. The Director will receive future promised
income based on the promissory note from APHC Inc., the parent company of Kimley Horn. APHC
Inc. will be a source of income until 12 months after the promissory note is satisfied. In addition,
the Director will continue to have a separate investment interest in APHC Inc. until the promissory
note is cleared.

In addition, an official has an interest in any parent, subsidiary, or other related business of
as source of income, as defined in Regulation 18700.2(b), which provides:

(b) Parent, Subsidiary, Otherwise Related Business Entity, defined.

(1) Parent - A business entity is a "parent" if it is a corporation that controls
more than 50 percent of the voting stock of another corporation. The parent
corporation is also a parent to any subsidiaries of the corporation that it
controls.

(2) Subsidiary - A business entity is a "subsidiary" if it is a corporation
whose voting stock is more than 50 percent controlled by another
corporation. The subsidiary corporation is also a subsidiary to any
corporation that controls its parent corporation.

(3) Otherwise related business entity. Business entities, other than a parent
corporation as defined in subdivision (b)(1), are otherwise related if:

(A) The same person or persons together direct or control each business
entity; or

(B) The same person or persons together have a 50 percent or greater ownership
interest in each business entity.

File No. A-20-029
Page No. 4

Because the Director has interests in APHC, Inc. and Kimley Horn is wholly owned by
APHC, Inc., the Director also has financial interests in Kimley Horn, which falls within the above
definition of "subsidiary." An official with a financial interest in a business entity also has an
interest in a parent of or subsidiary of the business entity or an otherwise related business entity.
(Regulation 18700.2(c).)

Foreseeability and Materiality - Business Entity and Source of Income

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision. An official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision.

Similarly, a reasonably foreseeable material effect on an official's interest in a business
entity including a source of income is material any time the interest is explicitly involved in the
decision including any decision in which the business entity bids on, or enters into, a contract with
the agency, or is identified as a subcontractor on a bid or contract with the agency, as well as any
decision in which the entity is the subject of any action taken by the agency that is directed at the
entity. (Regulations 18702.1(a) and 18702.3(a)(4).)

In this case, Kimley Horn would bid on ACTC projects, either directly or as a subcontractor,
as well as perform work under existing projects subject to oversight by ACTC staff. Based on the
facts provided, Kimley Horn is explicitly involved in the contemplated decisions. Thus, the
financial effect of the decision on Kimley Horn is both foreseeable and material, and the Act
prohibits the Director from taking part in these decisions. In regard to supervising Kimley Horn, we
are unable to determine whether the Director can supervise as a general matter. These
determinations are necessarily fact specific and disqualification under the Act can be determined
only in regard to specific decisions before the agency. However, because the Director may not
make, participate in making, or use his position to influence any decision with a reasonably
foreseeable and material effect on the Kimley Act, we caution that the Director supervising Kimley
Home may not be practicable.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

Zachary W. Norton

By:    Zachary W. Norton
       Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:aja

Horn submits a bid, or supervising Kimley Horn’s work if it is selected by ACTC?

Because the Director of Projects has a financial interest in Kimley Horn, he may not 
participate in any decisions involving either APHC Inc., the parent company of Kimley Horn and 
maker of the promissory note, and Kimley Horn, a wholly owned subsidiary of APCH, Inc.

In the Holzhaus Advice Letter, No. I-14-218, we said that an official had an investment

five-year note because a note is a debt instrument. (See also, the Gallogly Advice Letter, No. 
A-17-073, applying the rule to a promissory note.) 
 
• An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, except gifts or loans 
by a commercial lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms 
available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating $500 or more in value 
provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time

(2) Subsidiary - A business entity is a “subsidiary” if it is a corporation 
whose voting stock is more than 50 percent controlled by another 
corporation. The subsidiary corporation is also a subsidiary to any 
corporation that controls its parent corporation. 
(3) Otherwise related business entity. Business entities, other than a parent 
corporation as defined in subdivision (b)(1), are otherwise related if: 
(A) The same person or persons together direct or control each business 
entity; or

Kimley Horn disqualify him from participation in the process of reviewing submissions if Kimley 
Horn submits a bid, or supervising Kimley Horn’s work if it is selected by ACTC?

CONCLUSION: Because the Director of Projects has a financial interest in Kimley Horn, he may not 
participate in any decisions involving either APHC Inc., the parent company of Kimley Horn and 
maker of the promissory note, and Kimley Horn, a wholly owned subsidiary of APCH, Inc.
